data.title: Macquarie Global Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global markets, as represented by the MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index) (net), returned 7.62%, adding to gains from the first half of 2025. Technology stocks dominated globally amid continued optimism about artificial intelligence (AI). This buoyed the tech-heavy markets in the US, Taiwan, and Korea. In the US, strong earnings growth and resilient consumers combined with an anticipated US Federal Reserve (Fed) easing cycle helped sentiment, with a notable retail investor impulse behind growth names, including profitless ones. Outside the US, the performance of European markets was more mixed with a focus on fiscal policy in France and the UK and on geopolitics, with Russian incursions into Eastern Europe. Looking East, Asian markets were strong, propelled higher by Chinese technology stocks and AI-related names in South Korea and Taiwan. Japan’s stock market reached record highs, surpassing its 1989 peak.

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise indicated.

Within the Fund

For 3Q25, Macquarie Global Growth Fund (Formerly Delaware Ivy Global Growth Fund) Institutional Class shares posted a positive total return but underperformed the Fund’s benchmark, the MSCI ACWI (net).

Stock selection proved challenging across most sectors this quarter with impacts from select stocks owned in the Fund and several high flyers that were not owned. This was particularly true within the consumer discretionary sector, where Chinese internet consumer platforms rallied, alongside Tesla (not owned) in the US, while performance among the Fund’s luxury goods holdings was soft. Within tech, we had reduced our semiconductor exposure on strength, but the group saw continued outperformance amid AI tailwinds. In contrast, the debate continued for software, an area of exposure for the Fund that was challenged during the quarter. Within communication services, the Fund’s telecom stocks, normally defensive in nature, broke lower and trailed the benchmark while benchmark heavyweight Alphabet Inc. (an underweight) surged.

Among individual stocks, Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA (BBVA) (Spain), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (TSMC)(TSM)(Taiwan), and KLA Corp. (USA)(KLAC) were the strongest relative performers.

  • BBVA, a leading banking franchise across Spain and Latin America, saw shares rally amid robust economic growth in Spain, supporting lending growth (alongside falling interest rates). The company also initiated medium-term targets through 2028, which were well received by the market.
  • TSMC, the world’s leading semiconductor manufacturer, rose amid robust demand for chips due to rising computing intensity of models with growing usage and reasoning capabilities.
  • KLA is a supplier of semiconductor equipment, including inspection and metrology, critical for manufacturing semiconductors. The stock outperformed strongly in 3Q, driven by robust demand for semi equipment, in particular for advanced logic, high band memory (HBM) and advanced packaging, fueled by the AI infrastructure investments.

The greatest individual detractors were the Fund’s positions in Carrier Global Corp. (USA) (CARR) and SAP SA (SAP)(Germany), and an underweight to Tesla (USA) (TSLA).

  • Carrier Global is a worldwide leader in energy solutions, including heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC). Shares declined as the industry struggled to set proper guidance, resulting from a regulated refrigerant change in the residential HVAC market. We remain positive on the name given the company’s current transformation into a pure play HVAC and refrigeration company, which we believe should be an above-trend market over the long term.
  • SAP, the German enterprise software maker, declined amid broader software sector weakness despite strong underlying business growth. The outlook remains positive, in our view, with a continued product upgrade cycle augmented by growing AI opportunities.
  • Tesla, the multinational electric vehicle company, outperformed amid hype around its Robotaxi and Optimus potential. (Optimus is a general-purpose robotic humanoid under development by Tesla.)

Outlook

The outlook for global markets remains compelling, in our view. Starting with the US, the AI secular growth narrative continues to boost equity markets. At the same time, concerns are emerging around valuation, circular deals, and other external financing needs to fund this extraordinary infrastructure buildout. Away from AI, the “old” economy seems to be slowing down, with labor market weakness combined with continued tariff-linked uncertainty, although US consumers, for now, remain resilient. The Fed also began its rate-cutting cycle, which could support the economy and asset prices.

In Europe, political and geopolitical risks seem more contained today than at the end of 2Q25. The US began implementing the European Union trade deal in August, with a 15% tariff cap, which provides relief across industries, notably healthcare companies – an area of exposure for the portfolio. We also continue to see momentum for defense spending in Europe despite a dip for defense stocks in 3Q amid Ukraine security talks. As we previously wrote, NATO leaders agreed to increase defense spending to 5% of gross domestic product (or $1.5 trillion in additional annual spending across NATO), a target that looks reinforced by the absence of progress on the Russia-Ukraine front.

Another area of exposure has been industrials amid tailwinds from Germany’s €500 billion infrastructure program, which has the potential to buoy the domestic economy. Overall, earnings momentum has stabilized and consensus growth is improving for 2026, as investors turn more constructive, driven by attractive valuations, rising buybacks, and gradually improving macroeconomic conditions in Europe.

In Asia, with the exception of Japan, central banks have embraced a dovish stance, made possible by the Fed embarking on its own easing cycle. This should support economic activity across the region. Geopolitical concerns have also abated, removing an overhang on markets, although flare-ups are possible – India remains a sticky point while a truce with China has been extended. While Chinese technology stocks have momentum, the economy has much less. We will continue to look for ideas but maintain our valuation discipline. Another market of interest is Japan, where the outlook remains positive, with strong corporate earnings, a weak yen, and AI-related momentum.

Despite a challenging few months for the portfolio, it is important to remain grounded. We maintain our valuation discipline and continue to devote most of our time to assessing companies’ fundamentals. Our focus is on companies we believe have long-term compounding characteristics, unrecognized growth potential, and high-quality cyclicals. This should lead to a portfolio with a diverse set of exposures and risk characteristics, which primarily generates returns through stock selection as opposed to country or thematic exposure.

Global markets provide investors with exposure to the strongest businesses in the world regardless of the country of domicile. Considering that valuations are relatively high and certain parts of the market are at extremes, we believe a global approach to portfolio construction offers investors an advantage and more diverse exposure.

data.title: One Year On: The Rise Of The S&P Quality FCF Aristocrats Indices
data.description:
By Elizabeth Bebb

Launched Sept. 23, 2024, the S&P Quality FCF Aristocrats Indices track companies that consistently generate robust free cash flow (FCF) over a specific number of years. FCF, which represents the cash remaining after a business meets its operational costs and capital investments, serves as a key barometer of company quality.

The S&P Quality FCF Aristocrats Indices were initially introduced across two universes: the S&P 500® Quality FCF Aristocrats Index and the S&P Developed Quality FCF Aristocrats Index, which both require positive FCF for at least 10 consecutive years. Both indices have outperformed their respective benchmark universes over the one-year period since their launch and on a YTD basis in 2025. The S&P 500 Quality FCF Aristocrats Index notably outperformed during the tariff-related drawdowns, while the S&P Developed Quality FCF Aristocrats Index saw a slightly higher drawdown, reflecting the different responses to the economic backdrop across regions.

Over the long term, including hypothetical back-tested performance, both indices have consistently outperformed their benchmark universes, demonstrating strong absolute terms as well as impressive risk-adjusted performance.

An analysis of these indices across various macroeconomic environments, including hypothetical back-tested performance, shows that they have excelled in a range of regimes characterized by rising and falling inflation and growth. Historically, the S&P Developed Quality FCF Aristocrats Index outperformed or matched the S&P Developed Large MidCap in all four environments. The S&P 500 Quality FCF Aristocrats Index only underperformed the S&P 500 during periods of high growth and high inflation.

These indices aim to identify high-quality companies through FCF-based screening and selection metrics utilized in their methodology. Exhibit 4 examines the metrics that make up the FCF score for both indices, revealing that they exhibit higher return on equity (ROE), higher operating margins and lower financial leverage compared to their respective benchmark universes.

Quality can be assessed by various metrics; however, FCF has historically proven to be an effective lens for identifying high-quality companies. The S&P Quality FCF Aristocrats Indices have had a strong start, outperforming their benchmark universes since launch while also demonstrating positive risk-adjusted performance, robust fundamentals and defensiveness over longer back-tested periods.

data.title: Healthy Bull Or Bloated Bubble? A Stock Picker's Take
data.description:
By Tony DeSpirito

Are U.S. stocks approaching bubble territory or is the bull run able to press on? Active investor Tony DeSpirito is optimistic but says pockets of bubble-like exuberance could create mispricings ― making it “an exciting time for stock selection.” He suggests three areas that may be ripe for the picking.

With the S&P 500 Index (SPX) on pace to potentially return 20%+ for a third year running, many market observers are getting nervous. The last time this happened was during the internet bubble in the late 1990s.

Terms like “bubble” and “irrational exuberance” are making headlines. But are U.S. stock prices at or approaching levels inconsistent with their underlying fundamentals? Our view: Not overall, but some spots bear watching. As fundamental-based stock pickers, we see this setting up a favorable backdrop for stock selection.

Not your grandparents’ stock market

Price-to-earnings (P/E) ratios on the S&P 500 Index sat at nearly 24x at the end of October, roughly 50% above their long-term historical average.1 But we are not overly concerned.

Why? Because the index itself is not what it was 25 or 50 years ago. The S&P 500 today is dominated by mega-cap growth stocks. It is as concentrated as it ever has been, with our analysis finding the top 10 stocks accounting for 40% of its return profile as of October. Further review of data back to 1989 shows the index is the growthiest it has ever been, with growth stocks comprising 44% relative to value stocks at less than 8% of total composition.2 Given a historically large share of growth, we would expect the index to have a higher P/E ratio than it has historically. Most of the “Magnificent 7” mega-cap stocks carry what we would consider reasonable valuations for their superior earnings growth outlook.

Beneath the surface, we are finding plenty of very good large-cap stocks at attractive valuations ― bulls with room to run. The top line, dominated by a small group of big names, simply skews the average.

Bubbling up?

That said, we do observe some signs of irrationality, or speculative behavior, in the market today. Our review of factor returns year-to-date shows momentum stocks in the lead, a classic sign of performance chasing. Crowding in these positions can cut both ways, offering potential for above-market returns but also greater risk on a downturn.

We also find that unprofitable companies have outperformed the broader market, while low-volatility and dividend-paying baskets have lagged so far this year.3 At the same time, “meme stock” investing among retail investors guided by social media trends has gained popularity.

As shown in the chart below, retail and higher-frequency traders represent a much larger share of total stock trading volume relative to 15 years ago, increasing their influence on broad market movements as well as on individual stock volatility and pricing.

Rising irrationality?

Change in share of trading volume, 2010-2025

Source: BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from Bloomberg, as of Sept. 18, 2025. Chart shows the percent change in each group’s share of trading volume in U.S. stocks in Q1 2025 vs. Q1 2010, based on market maker 605 reports and estimates from Bloomberg Intelligence. High-frequency funds include quant funds that hold positions over minutes or less. Retail includes self-directed individual investors trading through both direct-to-consumer platforms and traditional brokerages.

While some fret over a trading environment dominated by non-traditional, less fundamentally oriented investors, we see this as an opportunity for skilled stock pickers to generate additional alpha, or above-market return.

Why? Because individual stock mispricings that can arise amid pockets of irrationality often present attractive buying opportunities.

Sectors ripe for the picking?

Against this backdrop, we highlight three sectors we believe may be particularly well suited for alpha generation by fundamental-based stock pickers.

1. Technology

We do not see tech and AI stocks in a bubble. This is not 2000. The earnings of many of the publicly traded AI-leveraged companies today justify their higher prices, in our view. That was not true 25-30 years ago when the dot-com bubble inflated and ultimately burst. Many high-flying companies then were unprofitable. And our review of the top 25 tech and tech-adjacent stocks by market cap in 2000 and 2025 shows the median valuation was nearly 85% higher then versus now.4

On the contrary, we see tech as an area of opportunity for stock pickers. Because AI is an innovation unlike any before, with implications throughout the economy, backward-looking algorithms are unlikely to provide a reliable guide to the next leg of AI earners. But fundamental analysts can look across the AI stack to identify potential sources of alpha as the technology and its use cases evolve. We look for underappreciated beneficiaries whose prospects may be obscured within the larger swell of this market-moving mega force.

2. Healthcare

Despite underperformance in recent years, we believe healthcare is well underpinned by structural tailwinds from aging populations and a growing need for medical care, as well as an innovation impulse. Overall valuations are low relative to the broad market, yet this is not a homogenous sector. Selectivity will define outcomes. Consider one stock in the HMO space that has appreciated 68% year-to-date while a competitor’s stock price fell 32%.5 Portfolios that owned the former and not the latter realized a dual benefit.

3. Financials

An easing regulatory environment for the financials sector is a pronounced shift relative to the regime in place since the 2008 Global Financial Crisis (GFC). This forms a new structural backdrop for stock selection. Companies with lower return on equity (ROE) should do disproportionately well relative to post-GFC outperformers that have less room to increase ROE and expand their earnings and market multiple.

The bottom line

We do not see a bubble in U.S. stocks today. Some areas of the market may be inflated by overexuberance and crowded positioning, but others have room for multiple expansion. As stated in our latest Equity Market Outlook, we believe leaning into the virtues of bottom-up stock analysis and selection can help to avoid the pitfalls of psychological contagion and crowded positioning that typically arise amid pockets of irrational exuberance.

In short, fundamental research focused on deep company analysis lends discipline and rationality to equity investing, virtues we find particularly relevant amid a pick-up in speculative behaviors.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock.

data.title: Comcast: Even Better Buying Opportunity With Double-Digit Returns Amid Market Overreaction
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered Comcast (CMCSA), I upgraded them to a Strong Buy following an even better entry level, stronger fundamentals, and improving risk-reward potential.

Right now, with the stock getting cheaper after beating estimates due mostly to broader market activity and several macro improvements, I am even more confident in the potential value that this very solid cash flow producer offers, developing the position while taking advantage of the market’s worries about broader factors such as rate cuts.

Internal Developments

Despite falling on earnings date, Comcast’s Q3 report beat expectations and came with fewer subscriber losses than expected in domestic video customers (with higher wireless line additions), while YoY data was impacted by the positives coming from the Paris Olympics last year.

What we can see, though, is the very solid free cash flow of $4.945 billion during Q3, which is 45.2% higher compared to Q3’24. This allowed the company to keep the very solid shareholder returns high, with $1.5 billion in buybacks and $1.2 billion in dividends, meaning a combined yield of ~2.73% for Q3 alone, while the TTM levels would bring the combined yield to a whopping ~12.35%, which is definitely not something to ignore, especially since it comes with a very sustainable ~67.31% based on the TTM free cash flow of $18.125 billion. The CFO had some comments regarding this and why they lowered the buybacks a bit, citing some near-term headwinds during the Q3 Earnings Release:

We do have some near-term headwinds, namely the EBITDA impact from our broadband repositioning, the onboarding of NBA rights and the spinoff of VERSANT and its associated EBITDA and free cash flow. For those reasons, we reduced our quarterly buyback pacing a touch to $1.5 billion in the quarter. I'd point out this remains one of the strongest absolute and percentage buybacks in our broader peer group. At the same time, our healthy dividend offers a yield that is multiple times the yield of the broader market. So we believe we are balancing strong returns back to shareholders with significant reinvestment in our business and doing so in the context of a balance sheet that provides cushion through any operating or macroeconomic environment.

Meanwhile, if we annualize the 9M FCF, we’re looking at about $19.82 billion this year, so it’s definitely possible to see even higher shareholder returns while they remain sustainable. They also mention a few more details during the Q3 Earnings Release:

And year-to-date, we have generated $14.9 billion in free cash flow. The increase in the quarter was driven by a tailwind in cash taxes from the new legislation along with favorable working capital timing, particularly around studio production spend and the comparison to the Olympics.

Financially, based on Comcast’s latest report, we see significant improvements, with higher current and non-current assets, while the total debt remained relatively unchanged (lower non-current, higher current). It’s also worth noting that they have ~$9.325 billion in cash and cash equivalents, which is definitely not a small sum, providing solid liquidity that can fund their higher-growth investments.

Something I mentioned before is the potential bid war around Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), which I’ve been adding hand over fist at an average of ~$8.09 per share, being up about 180% on it now. What I also mentioned is that Comcast could actually take advantage of this at a time when the Paramount Skydance (PSKY) takeover was reportedly looking less likely, which they actually did recently, allegedly hiring bankers for a potential bid.

Again, WBD comes with a massive library of IPs that rivals even Disney’s, potentially making any buyer a powerhouse in the industry overnight, not to mention HBO, studios, or other assets, which is why I think even the bigger players like Amazon (AMZN) or Apple (AAPL) should be interested instead of spending even hundreds of millions just for the rights to produce content from well-known IPs - see Amazon’s ~$250 million spent for the LOTR rights, not including the production/marketing itself.

Comcast’s advantage is that they are also a big player with very deep pockets (not rivaling Amazon or Apple, but still massive), also being a massive cash flow producer just like WBD. Any of these large players can take advantage of their size, refinance/repay some or even all of WBD’s debt if they want to (although it’s not really high-interest, just a lot even if they’re repaying it), and deliver massive synergies by being able to invest much more in these IPs.

Again, if Amazon initially spent over $1 billion for producing their LOTR Rings of Power series, I can see them easily being interested in spending many times more for the DC Universe, for example. Comcast can also integrate these IPs in their theme parks, though, potentially delivering even more synergies compared to one of the larger peers who mostly focus on streaming.

Still, Comcast kept advancing on other fronts, confirming that they are in talks to acquire ITV’s media and entertainment unit for $2.15 billion, potentially developing their British exposure through one of the UK’s largest and oldest commercial television broadcasters.

Macro Developments

The main, major development for a company like this has been rate cuts, with a new one announced in late October and hopes for another cut in December as well. For a company like Comcast, this can be great from several points of view, as the company has a sizable amount of debt that’s costing them ~$4.35 billion in annual interest (based on the TTM level).

Not only that, but they ultimately depend on discretionary budgets - especially in their higher-growth areas such as theme parks and streaming - so lower rates coming with increased spending and higher consumer budgets can be significant factors that can fuel improving numbers.

However, as the US Government recently went through what became its longest shutdown in history, it comes with implications for the future of rate cuts. Their near-term future is now more uncertain because the BLS also halted its data collection and publishing during this shutdown, while Powell even said that a December cut is not yet clear, and he cited worries about inflationary pressure and rising unemployment during the October meeting.

This caused the market to drop following the meeting, while a definitive “no” in December can continue this and build even more fear around the future of rate cuts in 2026.

That being said, Comcast’s management will have to navigate their transition in an even more unpredictable environment for the time being, with any delay in rate cuts hurting them directly while also potentially leading to even lower stock prices, meaning less access to external capital at a time when they could’ve taken advantage of a strong valuation to buy WBD’s assets using equity, for example.

About that, another opportunity that I’d like to mention is doing what WBD plans (or at least planned before the bid), meaning splitting their legacy cable business and the higher-growth segments into two, which is another catalyst that I identified a few years ago when building the position. I believe Comcast can also look into something similar - apart from the Versant spin-off - divesting part of the lower-margin assets to reduce some debt or invest in higher-potential areas, such as the ITV unit deal that’s in the making.

Updated Valuation

Given the better-than-expected cash flows, I’ll start with $15 billion in FCF in 2025, already accounting for the Versant spin-off lowering their cash flow while also being more conservative and accounting for higher CAPEX. This FCF is once again projected to grow at a conservative 4% CAGR for the first 5 years (thanks to rate cuts and streaming/parks plus their overall growth focus), followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, with a 2% terminal growth rate. I’ll go for a WACC of 7.5% (assumptions: ~11.4% cost of equity, 4.4% cost of debt), $89.74 billion in net debt, $555 million in noncontrolling interests, and 3.69 billion shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This results in an enterprise value of $306.39 billion, an equity value of $216.09 billion, and an intrinsic value per share of $58.56, which is significantly below the current stock price.

Conclusions

Comcast remains a strong buy in my opinion, with significant recovery potential as their turnaround and macro tailwinds unwind, while paying double-digit shareholder returns on top. There are definitely risks coming, especially from the quality of their services and falling traditional business combined with near-term macro headwinds, but they’re also able to make a very significant amount of money while being valued at very low levels that make the risk-reward very appealing.

data.title: Harding Loevner International Small Companies Equity Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
What's on Our Minds

Markets are a sea of variables, but some variables are more persistent than others. The International Small Companies Equity portfolio targets durable sources of return—quality, growth, and value—and each holding is meant to be at least a three- to five-year voyage. Momentum investing, by contrast, seeks to profit from recent price trends rather than underlying fundamentals. Momentum investors buy stocks because they have gone up or outperformed (or sell them because they have gone down or underperformed). While our holdings have historically delivered strong earnings growth and outperformance, they can require patience when market momentum works against them.

The portfolio’s performance was in line with the benchmark through the first half of 2025, but a gap opened in the third quarter due to an underexposure to higher-beta stocks that led the market rally. Higher-beta stocks tend to surge when risk appetite is strong but are less representative of the steady, lower-beta businesses we favor for their quality and resilience. Therefore, our stock selection was weak, consistent with, although not entirely explained by, an environment that rewarded recent winners. Shares of the highest-quality companies in the benchmark also underperformed those of the lowest quality by more than 1,000 basis points, while expensive stocks—of which many are higher-growth and higher-quality businesses—significantly trailed their cheaper counterparts.

Momentum-fueled markets emphasize what’s happening with stock prices in the moment, whereas our portfolio emphasizes company attributes and industry growth trends that we think are going to be relevant long term. The Health Care sector, among the portfolio’s biggest detractors for the quarter and year, is an area where we see a disconnect between short-term sentiment and long-term growth prospects.

Health Care has endured a turbulent five years. The global outbreak of the COVID-19 virus upended health-care systems and spending. Just as conditions began to normalize, more shocks arrived: a rapid increase in interest rates starting in 2022 tightened financial conditions and slowed funding for the development of early-stage drug development. More recently, U.S. policy uncertainty and proposed budget cuts have further constrained investment.

That said, funding is a supply problem. The long-term sources of demand haven’t changed: people are growing older, wealthier, and increasingly investing in their health. That’s a theme supportive of the outlook for many of the portfolio’s Health Care holdings. One example is Norway’s Medistim, whose equipment is used in heart surgeries; cardiovascular diseases are the leading cause of death globally.

People tend to associate the Health Care sector with large drug companies or insurers. After all, US pharmaceutical giants account for most of the sector’s 9% share of the MSCI ACWI Index. Health Care accounts for a similar portion of the international small-cap benchmark, but the profile of its constituents is different. Unlike large drug companies with established portfolios and predictable sales, small drugmakers generally focus on developing new therapies, and the absence of a sustainable source of cash flow can leave them with weak balance sheets. Therefore, the portfolio holds few drug companies and has generally gravitated toward Health Care equipment and supplies businesses because of their superior fundamentals, including strong cash flow generation and durable returns. Medistim is just one of many companies in this sub-industry that operate in defendable niches, where improved outcomes for patients, providers, and payers attributable to specific equipment also strengthens the market position for the supplier of that equipment.

A trade-off when investing in small-cap Health Care equipment and supplies companies is that customers’ orders can be cyclical because they are dependent on hospital and lab budgets, which fluctuate with interest-rate cycles and competing needs. However, high recurring revenues from an established base of repeat customers can help companies weather those cycles. Excluding pharmaceutical companies, two-thirds of the aggregate sales generated by the portfolio’s Health Care holdings are recurring.

Market Snapshot

The international small-cap benchmark rose 6.8%, in line with its large-cap counterpart. Materials and Energy were the best-performing sectors, amid higher prices for key commodities, including gold, as well as geopolitical uncertainty. Information Technology (IT) also outperformed, led by AI-related stocks. The Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples sectors lagged. In these sectors, the benchmark is dominated by countries that depend on commodity imports, such as those in Europe, which are hurt by concerns about cost inflation and its impact on consumer sentiment. Canada’s market surged due to its heavy exposure to Materials and Energy businesses. Europe, including countries outside the monetary union, gave back some gains from earlier in the year, when Germany announced its fiscal stimulus plan. Japan outperformed as Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba’s resignation and the subsequent Liberal Democratic Party leadership race boosted expectations for fiscal expansion, tax cuts, and labor reforms.

Take Sysmex (OTCPK:SSMXF)(OTCPK:SSMXY), which is a global leader in its niche and has 78% recurring sales. The Japanese company supplies diagnostic analyzers and the reagents that allow these devices to perform blood analysis, urinalysis, and other testing. Sysmex’s recurring sales come from its reagents and from servicing its installed devices. Increased testing, growth in emerging economies such as India, and the company’s large installed base should help revenue grow by mid to high single digits over the long term. Recent share-price weakness, mostly related to short-term growth challenges, including the transition to a new supply-chain and order-processing system, as well as policies in China that pressured health-care spending, gave us an opportunity to add this high-quality company at an attractive valuation.

At Carl Zeiss Meditec (OTCPK:CZMWF)(OTCPK:CZMWY), recurring revenue reached a high of 51% of total company sales for the first nine months of the year, up from 43% a couple years ago. The German company specializes in intraocular lenses, which are implants used in cataract surgery; it also supplies high-precision microscopes for surgeons performing procedures on very small structures, such as those in the brain or eye. Demand for its products has been soft this year, particularly in China. Management also said last month that it expects the global macroeconomic environment “to remain volatile” and doesn’t foresee a rapid recovery in spending on equipment or elective procedures. Long term, however, demographic trends look favorable for the global vision-care market, and Carl Zeiss’s strong competitive advantages—its technological leadership, brand trust, and large global installed base—position it to be a key beneficiary of the growing demand for eye surgeries and increasing number of people who can afford them. The stock looks attractively valued relative to that growth opportunity.

While recurring revenues have cushioned the downside for our Health Care holdings, our thesis is that equipment sales will eventually normalize in end markets where we see long-term structural growth. Medistim, whose equipment is used to monitor blood flow during open-heart and vascular surgeries, operates in a market that is already showing early signs of recovery. The trajectory of the company’s earnings and sales, which are linked to the number of procedures in which its equipment is used, follows a pattern familiar to many Health Care businesses in recent years: expectations grew rapidly during the pandemic before dropping in 2022, as macroeconomic challenges reverberated throughout the sector. This year, though, utilization and orders of its equipment have improved. The underlying need for its equipment also isn’t going away. Rather, Medistim’s main challenge is growing its addressable market. The company is investing in a sales and service network and reducing its reliance on third-party suppliers, moves that we think make sense.

The stocks of companies that provide coronary-surgery equipment and blood analyzers are much less likely to get swept up in extreme market momentum than, say, a company with a popular weight-loss drug or one that’s spending billions on new data centers for artificial intelligence (AI). However, as long-term-oriented shareholders, we aren’t looking for the next hot stock or the binary risks that come with that type of investing. Health Care is an area where leaning into weaker sentiment has enabled us to add strong businesses poised for durable growth.

Portfolio in Focus

The portfolio has a significant underweight in Materials, which was the benchmark’s top-performing sector this quarter. Within Materials, metals and mining stocks outperformed, especially gold miners, as gold prices continued to set new highs. The portfolio doesn’t hold any gold-mining companies, for good reason.

Gold mining has an unattractive industry structure. Mining is capital intensive, and it has high regulatory barriers, but not so high that smaller mining companies cannot enter; high gold prices also attract new entrants. Therefore, rivalry can be fierce as numerous companies compete to find resource-rich land, which contributes to higher exploration costs. Bargaining power of suppliers can also be quite strong. Smaller mining companies often lack leverage with critical suppliers of equipment—companies that tend to be significantly larger than them—as well as with labor, all of which leads to higher costs and reduced profitability. Lastly, while gold is a hard commodity, its role as a substitute for other financial assets, as evidenced by recent central bank buying to diversify cash reserves, can lead to volatility that is detached from the underlying supply and demand of the commodity.

These dynamics, in our view, make equities linked to gold prone to long-term underperformance. We do not chase momentum in anything, and certainly not in commodity price cycles. Instead, the portfolio seeks businesses with durable competitive advantages, strong balance sheets, and rational industry structures—an approach that nonetheless can be uncomfortable when surging gold prices lift gold-mining stocks.

Unlike gold miners, IT-services companies benefit from an industry structure that is more favorable. However, the portfolio’s IT-services holdings, of which there are six, have weighed on relative returns for several quarters.

As discussed in last quarter’s letter, IT services is a broad umbrella, and each of the six companies we hold has distinct strengths because of its region, end markets, or technological expertise. While each has also faced challenges this year, it’s important to distinguish between secular concerns and cyclical ones.

The secular concern is that AI could shrink the industry’s overall revenue by changing how clients buy IT services. The service that many of these companies offer is people—software engineers who build and test custom applications. Payment is for “time and materials”, meaning how long a project takes and how many engineers are assigned to it. As AI boosts the productivity of software engineers, clients may expect projects to require fewer hours and fewer engineers. That concern is valid, though more valid for some companies than others. Among the portfolio’s holdings, Globant (GLOB) appears to be the most exposed, given its focus on software-development services and heavy reliance on time-and-materials contracts. The company is responding by shifting toward a subscription model, where customers pay monthly for access to “AI Pods,” its suite of AI-powered tools. The approach is ambitious and still in its early stages, with the goal of diversifying revenue over time. There are risks in how it executes this strategy, though we think Globant is capable of success and will be monitoring developments closely.

The secondary challenges to the industry are cyclical. There has been a reduction in demand for IT services across the board, following a spending binge the last few years as the pandemic incentivized many businesses to invest in digital technologies. The recent macroeconomic environment has led to more cautious budgeting, and some projects are taking a backseat to higher-priority AI-related work.

The challenges for Reply (OTCPK:RPYTF) and Bechtle (OTC:BHTLF) mostly fall into this cyclical bucket, and we think neither is likely to see its market position weakened by AI. Reply has built a workforce with specialized knowledge of machine learning and generative AI, and the company already has an array of AI offerings, putting it ahead of rivals. It is also much less reliant on time-and-materials contracts than many of them. Meanwhile, Bechtle is relatively insulated from AI disruption because it is primarily a technology reseller and systems integrator, with strong competitive advantages in its home market of Germany. In other words, whenever Microsoft or IBM rolls out new software or hardware, clients in Germany go to Bechtle for help installing it.

No one knows yet the long-term impact AI will have on industries and the economy. The adoption of any new technology is a journey, not a single event. For now, most companies are still figuring out where AI can lower costs and lift productivity and how to measure the benefits. As everyone reassesses their IT needs, the large, long-dated contracts that once provided the IT-services industry with predictable revenues are being replaced by many smaller, shorter deals. We view this phase as transitory. Over time, as the learning curve progresses, AI needs and use cases should become clearer, enabling greater visibility of project scope and timelines. This should allow earnings growth for IT-services companies to recover and support higher share prices.

Moreover, recent AI-related news involving one of our other IT holdings demonstrates how quickly market perceptions can shift in times of rapid technological change. OpenAI, whose generative-AI models have propelled the race for computing infrastructure, has been striking large deals with companies such as NVIDIA, Microsoft, and Oracle to secure chips and processing power for its models. On October 6, it announced a new partnership with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), which may bode well for our new Dutch holding Besi, whose advanced-packaging technology enables chipmakers to pack more computing power into less space. Besi is an early leader in hybrid bonding, a type of advanced packaging used by AMD. Besi’s shares joined AMD’s in rallying the day the OpenAI partnership was announced.

It's a reminder that markets are still trying to piece together information about the evolving AI industry and the role that smaller companies will play. The uncertain environment underscores the importance of staying diversified and focusing on financially strong businesses with enduring competitive advantages.

Performance and Attribution

The International Small Companies Equity composite fell 0.9% gross of fees in the third quarter, compared with a 6.8% gain in the MSCI All Country World ex US Small Cap Index. For the year, the composite rose 18.1% gross of fees, trailing the index’s 26.1% gain.

Portfolio Attribution by Sector

About half the portfolio’s relative shortfall came from IT Sector, including a significant detractor within technology hardware and equipment as well as broad weakness across software and services. As discussed above, stock selection in Health Care and Materials also detracted. This was slightly offset by a helpful underweight in Real Estate and a few bright spots in other sectors.

Top contributors to relative performance:

  • Within Communication Services, Airtel Africa (OTCPK:AAFRF)(OTCPK:AARTY) was a strong contributor. Earnings exceeded expectations, partly due to tariff increases in Nigeria and improved performance in Francophone markets. Markets also have been anticipating the public listing of its mobile-money business by mid-2026.
  • The portfolio doesn’t have any holdings in Real Estate, a sector that continued to lag the benchmark, especially in European markets.
  • Our only Utilities holding, France-based Rubis (OTCPK:RUBSF), outperformed following reports that suitors are circling the fuel distributor.
  • In Energy, Dialog Group Berhad (OTCPK:DGUPF) and Romgaz (OTC:SLTQY) both outperformed the sector and benchmark. Shares of Romgaz, Romania’s largest natural gas producer, have climbed as Europe pivots away from Russian fuel; the company also has a major project starting in 2027 that could nearly double its current production. Shares of Malaysia-based Dialog recovered due to new storage contracts that helped stabilize earnings in its fiscal fourth quarter, after impairments weighed on results in the previous quarter.

Top detractors from relative performance:

  • In IT, LEM Holding (OTCPK:LMHDF), a Swiss maker of transducers used to measure electrical parameters, fell sharply after it reported weak results and pointed to pricing pressure and greater competition. Shift, a Japanese provider of outsourced software-testing services, despite beating consensus earnings estimates for the quarter, underperformed amid growing concerns about the threat of AI to software and services companies.
  • Carl Zeiss Meditec and DiaSorin (OTCPK:DSRLF) were among the portfolio’s weakest Health Care holdings. DiaSorin, an Italian maker of diagnostic tests, faced renewed concerns about compressed reimbursements for immunological and chemistry-based assays.
  • In Materials, Fuchs Petrolub, a German maker of specialty lubricants, cut its sales and profit guidance, citing muted demand from customers affected by US tariffs as well as a slowdown in European industrial production.
  • Relative returns were weak across the Financials sector. GMO Payment Gateway (OTCPK:GMYTF), Japan’s leading provider of cashless payment-processing services, had a major customer terminate its contract. While the hit to earnings in GMO’s fiscal year that ended in September was smaller than expected, markets grew concerned that the full effects of the loss will be felt later.

Portfolio Attribution by Region

Stock selection was particularly weak in the European Monetary Union (EMU) and Japan. Allocations in Frontier Markets and Emerging Markets (EMs) were additive.

Top contributors to relative performance:

  • In Frontier Markets, aside from Romgaz, Vietnamese steel producer Hoa Phat Group, rose after the country imposed duties on some Chinese steel imports, raising hopes for firmer domestic pricing.
  • In EMs, an underweight in India helped, as the country underperformed the benchmark due to concerns over US tariffs.
  • Mexico was also a relative bright spot. Grupo Herdez (OTCPK:GUZOF), the country’s leading packaged-food manufacturer, rose after agreeing to sell a 25% stake in its 50-50 joint venture to its partner, US spice giant McCormick.

Top detractors from relative performance:

  • In the EMU, Reply and Carl Zeiss Meditec were significant detractors.
  • The largest detractor in Japan was Shift. Ariake (OTCPK:AKEJF), Japan’s largest maker of livestock-derived natural seasonings, also weighed on returns, as softer restaurant demand in Japan and China resulted in slower sales growth.
  • In Europe ex EMU, LEM Holding was the largest detractor. Shares of YouGov, a UK based supplier of online market research and data analytics, also fell amid slow growth and macroeconomic pressures.
  • The portfolio’s sole Canadian holding, Kinaxis (OTCPK:KXSCF), a provider of supply-chain management software, reported weak growth in its professional-services business and an outlook that was softer than expected.

data.title: Time To Take Profits On Celestica Stock (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Insufficient Upside Leads to Downgrading

I rarely downgrade stocks based on their seemingly excessive valuations, but when it comes to Celestica (CLS) stock, which I still like, I have no other choice but to downgrade it to "Hold" from my previous "Buy" rating because it has already been priced too expensively to give prospective investors a sufficient margin of safety. After my updated analysis on the valuation, I've got only slightly above 1% of upside. I think that Celestica stock's multiple expansion has gone too far, and it may be finally time to trim it if you're holding.

Looking At Celestica's Recent Financials

Before diving into my valuation calculations, I think we need to take a look at the firm's business momentum first. With $3.19 billion in sales (+28% YoY), which Celestica had in Q3, it managed to beat the consensus by 5.1%, while on the bottom line, the adjusted EPS rose by 52% YoY thanks to the ongoing shift toward the HPS ("High-Performance Solutions") sub-segment within the CCS segment and the subsequent margin expansion.

As a result of all this, CLS beat the consensus on the bottom line by 6%, according to Seeking Alpha. The market and even the management itself couldn't have expected the strength of the continued hyperscaler ramps, which drove up the demand for HPS solutions and helped CLS expand on both margins and absolute sales figures above consensus. This has led to another positive guidance adjustment: The company now expects 31% revenue growth in FY2026, with EPS rising by ~39% amid yet another cycle of margin expansion.

As CLS pruned lower-margin programs at ATS and focused more on what really grew—the HPS—the latter sub-segment started to account for ~44% of its consolidated total revenue, which is up from 8% just a year ago. This revenue mix shift turned out to be much faster than I initially expected when I first started covering the stock on Seeking Alpha.

In my previous article, however, I was seeing the underlying trend, and I assumed that by 2027, the HPS part of its portfolio could reach 70-80% of consolidated sales:

Now the HPS sub-segment delivers ~43% of the entire sales pie, and it was ~8% last year. And if we simply extrapolate the current mix shift trajectory further into the next year or two, even at a declining pace, Celestica should be getting about 70-80% from the HPS sub-segment by 2027. It's a higher-margin niche that so far contributes ~8% in adjusted EBIT margin, so with the above-suggested revenue share forecast that I've just pointed out, Celestica's margin expansion story should stay with us for longer.

Source: From my previous article on CLS stock

The problem for CLS is that its HPS isn't that high-margin - the CCS overall made about 8.3% in EBIT margin for Q3.

It's a massive uptick of about 80 bps on a YoY basis for CCS, but there's a natural limit to Celestica's margin expansion ahead. They can probably reach 9% next year on this metric, but they're mainly a hardware provider, so the cost side will always be hard to manage for them.

I liked to see that they're expanding their manufacturing footprint in Texas and Thailand, where the capacity will be backed by multi-year volume commitments from customers like Dell Technologies (DELL), Hewlett-Packard Enterprise (HPE), and IBM (IBM), to name a few. Over the next few years, CLS isn't going to spend more than 2.5% of its sales on CAPEX, based on the commentary, so I see no red flags to their FCF generation capacity, especially given the rising market share in the niche. With the rise in AI spending, Celestica's top line is set to grow further, and I'm not sure that today's revenue projections, even after their recent post-earnings positive revisions, correctly reflect the whole opportunity.

So far, my analysis sounds bullish, so why am I downgrading then? It's all about the valuation and the lack of further upside that I see ahead.

Celestica's Valuation Update After The Rally

One of the core reasons why CLS has risen so much in recent years - aside from its superior financial growth - was the ongoing margin expansion. A simple chart, which I made in YCharts below, can prove it:

Now, assuming that CCS is reaching its peak margin already, or that it's going to be harder for it to gain as much as it did in 2023-2025, one of the levers for multiple expansion should be gone soon. At least it's the way I see the current setup.

As I've already noted above, I'm not suggesting the CCS segment is supposed to see its margins contracting from today's levels. It would sound like nonsense to me, given the market share gains and the rising AI CAPEX from hyperscalers. But what I think is going to happen is some sort of margin expansion pace staleness. In other words, Celestica's margin expansion has its limits because of the nature of the firm's operations, and now, it seems to be nearing its peak levels (the closer it gets to 9-10% on the forward basis).

Assuming that the market is a forward-looking machine, I think it can start to price this in, and if it happens indeed, the stock is unlikely to move much higher until the actual results start to adjust the current valuation.

And speaking about the valuation now, it looks too expensive already. The market expects $8.37 in adjusted EPS for FY2026, which is a 2% premium on top of the management's guidance. But even the embedded premium suggests an implied P/E ratio of almost 40x for FY2026:

Back in September, when I looked at the consensus figures last time, the consensus was expecting $6.72 in FY2026 EPS. Now it's $8.37, and I think we'll likely see 15-20% above that figure in consensus when CLS closes 1H FY2026. This gives me a prospective $9.834 in EPS for the next year.

As last time, I still think that CLS's fair P/E multiple at this growth pace is closer to the 34-35x range. Trading right at the middle of that range, CLS is already fairly valued to me.

Target EPS FY2026 * Target P/E multiple FY2026 = Price Target

$9.834 * 34.5x = $339.27/share

The current price is $335.50/share

The resulting upside is ~1.12% if my assumptions are close to being true

Final Words

Since CLS reported for its fiscal Q3 in late October, its stock price has adjusted higher amid stable broader market conditions as the analysts and investors quickly reassessed its growth prospects with the new data in hand. But then the rally started to slow down, and CLS went into a consolidation phase, which I believe can last longer than most bulls may be expecting.

With less margin expansion ahead, CLS's multiples are unlikely to grow as they did throughout 2021-2025. I assume that now CLS will have to grow out of its multiples in the next few years. I don't cancel out the business growth prospects. In fact, the shift toward HHP is still going smoothly, and the rise of AI CAPEX among big tech firms is a strong bullish signal, which means that the top-line growth isn't done yet. But I'd like to see CLS entering into a correction from its current levels. Perhaps the $345-365/share is its new resistance zone, and CLS should find its support before trying to break through it.

With all that in mind, I decided to downgrade my rating on CLS to "Hold". I'm holding myself, but I have trimmed a lot in recent days.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Aegon: We Are Interested In The 7% Baby Bond
data.description:
Aegon Ltd. (AEG) is a leading insurance company with a broad portfolio of assets and solid financials. In this article, we examine the company and its baby bond (AEFC), which caught our attention due to its yield-to-maturity of nearly 7%. We will examine if this particular bond presents "alpha" based on the company's basic metrics as well as some sector comparisons.

Company Basic Information

  • Total Assets: approximately $317.6 billion
  • Current market capitalization: close to $11.85 billion
  • Total Balance Sheet Equity: ~ $11.05 billion
  • Debt: close to 5 billion
  • Portfolio:

  • Historical performance:

AEG's 5-year total return on NAV is approximately 25.71%, and its 10-year total return is close to 5.07%.

  • Credit ratings:

Baby bond

AEG has one baby bond (AEFC): The bond is high-duration, trades below par, and has close to 24 years to maturity with a maturity date on 12/15/2049. It is rated with credit ratings by S&P: "BBB-" and by Moody's: "Baa2", so it falls into the "investment-grade" category. Since our software is based on data from QuantumOnline, the rating is not updated, and some of you may be misled. Below are the current Moody's ratings for AEFC:

A total of 37 million shares of AEFC were issued on 10/15/2019, each with a par value of $25, resulting in gross proceeds of $925 million. At the IPO date, the spread over the 20-year Treasury yield was approximately 3.04%, and currently the spread is about 2.29%. The bond pays an annual interest distribution of 5.10%, or $1.28 annually, distributed quarterly at $0.31875 per share.

The yield to maturity of AEFC is approximately 7.03%. For those interested in our yield calculation method, we determine the internal rate of return based on the expected cash flows of the bond. This approach includes the market price, periodic coupon payments, and the final principal repayment at maturity, which allows us to calculate XIRR for a more accurate yield estimate. Below you can see a comparison of the different yields at different potential AEFC prices.

Comparison With Peers

Below is shown a picture with a comparison between AEFC and other fixed-rate exchange securities with high duration, and rated as "investment-grade" by the credit agencies. It shows that AEFC ranks among the highest-yielding securities :

And if we compare it only to its peers in the insurance sector, we see that the bond outperforms with the highest yield:

The real value of the bond cannot be seen in comparison with exchange-traded debt at the moment, as there is a trend of widening spreads to the OTC bonds. This is a very rare occasion, and the norm is for the exchange-traded products to be constantly overvalued. I personally find this to be very unusual and full of "alpha." To show the true value of these bonds, we need to compare them with the overall bond market.

OTC bonds

AEG's OTC bonds are rated with one notch higher credit rating than its baby bond: "Baa1". The highest-yielding bond among the issues offers a yield to maturity of around 5.77%, which is 1.24 percentage points lower. If AEFC were to trade at a similar yield, its fair value would be approximately $23.50 - implying a potential upside of about 17% from the current level.

Now, let's look at the other over-the-counter bonds in the insurance sector with similar credit ratings to our bond (we choose those with credit ratings from Baa1 to Baa3):

The highest-yielding bond has a yield to maturity of close to 6.3%, or 0.73% lower than AEFC. Let's do the comparison again - if AEFC were to trade at the same yield, it would be priced at nearly $21.80, or around 9% potential appreciation.

Conclusion

AEFC has a high yield and an investment-grade credit rating, making it a solid choice for investors seeking income from high-duration bonds. Compared to other exchange-traded bonds and to the OTC bonds in the insurance sector, AEFC offers a competitive yield to maturity, making it an attractive investment opportunity. The Alpha is hidden in the fact that most of the time these exchange-traded products narrow their spread to the OTC bonds, even if lower-rated.

data.title: Manulife: Superb Performance, I Am Out (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I consider myself a bit of an "insurance buff". My investments in undervalued insurance companies can be said to have followed me throughout my entire Seeking Alpha "career", starting out with Unum (UNM) way back when. Most of the insurance investments I have made have seen between 50-300% rates of return since I invested in them, most between 60-180%. The insurance company that I am going to cover in this update has been making close to 90% RoR since my last article back in 2023. If you frequently follow my work and you do not have access to me directly through an IG, you might ask what you should do when I have not been updating for a company for over 2 years, and when things may have changed.

My stance is generally that, if I have not been posting an update, the old thesis remains. So it is with Manulife. My last article was here, in 2022; my "main" investment article, where I put most of the capital to work, was in late 2022. This is the one you see below in terms of returns, making the 130%+ returns from Manulife a bit of an "upper mid-range" return as I consider things.

Not bad at all, and certainly market-beating - which is obviously what I go for. Otherwise, what is the point of investing individually and not going for an index fund?

In this article, I will do above all one thing - I will show you why I sold MFC in November, and am selling the final portion of my stake as of this piece.

I have recently been covering a number of companies with updated articles - Enel (OTCPK:ENLAY) being a recent coverage, where I have significant stakes, which, due to excellent returns, have grown significantly. This is the case for MFC as well. This is a 2.5% position at this time, which is very big for my portfolio. I do not usually go above 2.5% in any position.

Selling this, and adding to my "cash pile" of over 30% is a hard choice. Obviously, for everything I sell, I get less dividends - but due to a mix of reasons I will go through here, I cannot justify holding this any longer.

Manulife - Upside Is Limited Going Forward

This should not come as a surprise. In my latest article, I gave the company a PT of around $23-$24, implying a valuation target of $31/share. That is also when I sold the first portion, and now, at $33/share for the NYSE, I am selling the rest.

This has nothing to do with the company's fundamentals. Manulife is an A-rated L&H insurer. L&H insurers are some of my favorites. Why? Because unlike P&C, they require less capital for their assets, underwriting, and risks. L&H instead involves longer-term guarantees, which makes it more of an "asset-light" operation compared to P&C. Instead of focusing on risks associated with property damage, liability, and GI questions, we are talking about an underwriting focus on life and investments.

When I bought MFC, the entire market was still severely undervaluing insurance companies - not just MFC. That is why it was so easy to make 130%+ RoR. It was a "set it and forget it" sort of investment. It can be argued that the company has been highly valued for over a year at this point, but I have not been focusing on rotation, and the market has not been as highly valued as it is right now.

So two components are guiding my decision to sell. First off, recent results, which to me imply that while growth is possible, it will likely be more difficult. Secondly, the entire market is currently so overvalued that I do not necessarily want to be in it any longer in investments where I have high rates of return.

When I bought MFC, the company was in a position of higher risk. MFC has addressed its risk profile through a number of valuable reinsurance transactions, reducing exposure to low-return blocks of long-term care policies and so-called valuable annuities. This is a bit of "insurance speech" - what it means essentially is that Manulife, as an insurance company, buys insurance for its own policies. The ceding company (what it is called), Manulife in this case, transfers some of the financial burden for catastrophic losses to a reinsurer.

Remember what happened with Unum and its LTC blocks, which was crucial to its crash a few years ago? It is related, and MFC is preventing this from happening, in essence. The reduction in risk is down to only 10% now in 2025, and that is from an almost 25% exposure back in 2017. These reinsurance transactions free up capital that MFC needs to hold in reserve to meet regulatory requirements - in total, 18 billion CAD was freed up this way. Much of this capital it freed up was returned to shareholders through buybacks.

But 'hold on,' you might say.

Was that really the best idea?

Yes and no (in my humble opinion). When this started, MFC was clearly undervalued by every reasonable metric. It made sense then. It has not, in my view, made sense for over a year now.

The company's fundamentals are extremely solid. It is the third-largest insurer in the Pan-Asian space. If you do not know or understand the Asian insurance space, spend some time reading up, because the company's operations outside of it are relatively insignificant by comparison. To give you an idea, in 2024, over 70% of new business came from Asia. Whether Asia goes well or not will decide how Manulife goes.

However, here we start to see trouble. Manulife's new Asian business might be growing, but margins are not. MFC has been pushing more of its low-return savings products, which make for easy sales but do not really give much. The company's targets include a switch to selling higher-return products - health and protection - as well as growing its high-net-worth business more.

But how? MFC wants to target 50% of its new business value from things like this. However, in these segments, competition is fierce. It is not like there are customers in these spaces without options for L&H insurance or products. The company's RoE target for Asia is 21% on a forward basis.

I am very dubious that this will be achieved - neither the historians nor peers support this target. Even if peers did, it would be a false equivalency to say that the company could achieve it just because X did - it takes years to build such things.

In other targets, I do believe that between mixing buybacks and organic growth, a 7-9% EPS growth could be achieved, the target being 10-12% in core EPS. Overall, I would go on record as characterizing MFC's targets as very optimistic.

I do not want to take away from the company's successes, especially not with 3Q25 results. The company has, very capably, moved away from its risk exposure in a way that I believe has vaccinated MFC from any massive crash in LTC. That is good. Higher returns because of this, with lower returns, are likely. Growth in Asia seems good as well and is likely to drive volumes going forward. Also, aside from Asia, Canadian markets are attractive for the company.

However, there is a risk side to this company, which is a very strong argument against further investing, and where I would see selling as favorable. Read more in my "risks & considerations" portion below.

For now, 3Q25 beat analyst targets - and in some cases, my own as well. Good momentum across the business, with record earnings in APAC as well as the company's other areas (like Canada). Negative trends are certainly there - I would point to the company's significant net outflows in asset management - over 6B CAD, which is a turnaround from net inflows both YoY and sequentially.

But new business growth delivered upside, and the company's book value was up as well. But regardless of how good the quarter was, I would not lose sight of the macro here. The company itself acknowledges the changing macroeconomic landscape with increased competition in the mix and distribution.

While the company's strategy is well-suited to guarantee, in my view, that things are unlikely to go down from here, that is not sufficient for long-term appeal at this valuation. There is no evidence that I can see of a case of intangible assets due to "branding" in the industry - customers buy insurance based on price, and I belong to the group that believes that scale advantages do not really apply to L&H as they do to P&C. Why? Because their operating cost base has fundamental characteristics of variability through commissions. The L&H fundamentals, where underwriting basics are far more stable (excepting things like COVID-19) in terms of calculating morbidity, comorbidity, and lapse rates, mean that competitive advantage here is very difficult.

MFCs and competitors' much-lauded AI investments will, of course, add some efficiency to the playbook, but one thing I do not often see is calculations of those savings net of cost investments over the lifetime of their investment. I see an upside from claims adjusting rates, but let me put this question to you as of 3Q25.

How do you believe that industry pricing rates in an AI-oriented L&H insurance industry will fare when most players seem to be doing it? I believe this will lead to further competition, new entrants, and further competition. In essence, I believe that the mere use of AI will "compete away" any cost advantage that AI was meant to give in the first place.

And if MFC starts issuing policies that are on the generous side to attract customers? Well, in that case, this opens the company up to loss risk in a market downturn. That is what we saw with companies like Unum. Why? Because Insurance companies do not know the true costs of their products until many years after they (often) have stopped selling them. Do you believe that Unum intended to take on all that risk that LTC blocks turned out to have? They did not - at the time, that was not the risk they saw.

Remember, middle management at insurance has financial and personal incentives to chase growth - bonuses and incentives are tied to this. I paint this statement with a broad brush - because I do not have data to support that MFC's managers do have this incentive specifically. This is something that has been amply covered in research papers like this.

So while 3Q25 was an excellent quarter, and I spent a lot of time above here discussing risk in ways that I do not usually do in an update article, there is a reason for this discussion. I want to provide a backdrop for the valuation section below, and the danger I see here.

Valuation - Manulife Is Not Very Attractive Here

While company risks have increased, valuation remains the deciding factor of why I am neutral, and pushing towards rotation here. I tend to go "SELL" when returns are negative (prospective), but that is not the case here.

I believe Manulife can manage a double-digit 10x P/E, which is also its 10-year average, and a good average (higher-end) in the sector of insurance. Even if we take an 8-9% EPS growth rate into account for the 2026-2029E period, however, at 2027E the company currently manages less than 7% per year.

Not only is that less than my hurdle, it is significantly less than other investments I am making at this time. Peers could be an argument for investing, but almost every peer is currently overvalued as well. For this specific subsegment, I would consider Great-West and Sun Life the two closest - both of them are overvalued by similar numbers, and they even have better yields than Manulife. Remember, my yield on cost for this company is over 7% due to my low entry.

So, without appeal from forecasts and without appeal from peers, I would consider this entire sector to be overvalued. That is why I, with the exception of Lincoln National (LNC) and Legal & General (OTCPK:LGGNY), am currently mostly out of insurance.

I consider the company to have a fair value of about $32, but with a PT of $25/share, justified by lower future growth than expected, and the market overvaluation we currently have.

Over the past few years, I have been able to make incredible returns from this sector - truly very good, in several investments. Many of those are now realized, meaning I have sold the stocks and holdings. This method of investing works for me. You may be a "buy and hold forever" investor. That is obviously fine - it is what I used to do as well.

Risks & Considerations are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The increase in valuation, the premiumization if you will, has resulted in the risks for the investment coming clearly to the forefront here. Some are smaller risks - like the company's asset allocation. Unlike many insurers, MFC has a high exposure to equities as opposed to fixed income. This can become a risk during a market downturn, as it has for others in the past. It is the reason most insurance businesses stick to fixed income, more stable.

Secondly, the company's core market is a very competitive place. Over 50-70% of the reason why I bought MFC was the valuation, not because MFC is somehow better than everyone else at what it does. It really is not. And this competitiveness makes it hard for MFC to find growth cheaply, which is what would be required to hit its target. Ergo, I do not believe targets will be hit by MFC.

There are some smaller blips on the radar - I could see a scenario of margin compression in some of MFC's retirement segments - but this is really just a note next to the book that is the valuation and the industry average here, which I believe constitute the main investment risks for this company.

These are the reasons I do not increase my targets, nor do I accept a higher premium.

Thesis

  • MFC was one of the more undervalued and high-yielding L&H insurance operations on the market, coupled with superb fundamentals, a class-leading credit rating, and excellent 3-5-year prospects about 3-4 years ago. That is no longer the case. As I see it, the investment has now fully matured, and I believe it is time to rotate.
  • Lower growth rates coupled with general market pressures and failure to hit lofty growth goals are going to be the main risks for why I believe this company is going down. If invested now, I expect no higher target than 5-8% per year, and that is insufficient for me at this time.
  • Based on this, I view MFC as a "HOLD" at a multiple of 9-10x with a fair value of about $32, but with a PT of $25/share, upgraded for some of the upsides, achievements, and buybacks, but no higher than that.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically is not enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $25/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me. I rate the company a "Hold" as of November 2025.

data.title: Clear Horizon: Emerging Markets Shine Amid Uncertainty
data.description:
Emerging markets offer a “clear horizon” amid developed market uncertainty—higher yields, lower volatility, resilient fundamentals, and China’s strong yuan keep EM assets shining.

Key Takeaways:

  • Developed markets face uncertainty, while emerging markets offer higher yields and lower volatility.
  • EMBX 30-day SEC yield: 6.4%†
  • IMF highlights EM resilience; fiscal pressures in developed markets support EM and gold.
  • China’s stable, strengthening yuan underpins EM strength and global trade shifts.

The VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMBX) was up 0.91% in October, compared to up 1.29% for its benchmark. Year-to-date, the fund is up 16.46%, compared to 14.51% for its benchmark, the 50% J.P. Morgan Government Bond Index - Emerging Markets Global Diversified (GBI-EM) and 50% J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index (EMBI) and 7.81% and 7.80% for the Global Agg and 10-year treasuries, respectively. As of October 31, 2025, the fund had an average yield to worst (YTW) of 8.3%, a carry of 6.7%, and a duration of 5.3 years. As of November 11, 2025, the 30-day SEC yield was 6.4%. In the past 5 years, the fund has returned 5.06% per year, compared to 2.55% for its benchmark, negative 2.03% and negative 2.52% for the Global Agg and 10-year treasuries, respectively. Argentina was the biggest outperformer again. In October, we were simply overweight, whereas earlier outperformance was due to not owning it for the bond price collapse two months ago as well as buying the collapse. Our overweight in South Africa local currency continues to shine and we saw an excellent rally in Bolivia USD bonds following important elections. Romania and Saudi were underperformers. We have pulled in our horns after very strong performance. Local currency exposure is lower at 49%, and we are reducing overweight positions in selected USD and local-currency markets.
